text_clean: Amazing ruins of the ancient holy city of Incas. The place doesn't need any explanation, it's among 7 world wonders and very well known. However I believe the local authorities did exploited this place too much without caring too much about the experience of the visit. Although the daily capacity of visitors is capped this place looks way to busy and filled with people who are just there to make selfies. My suggestion is to reach it via Inca trail, even just the last piece of the trekking, so that you can see it from the top of Porta del Sol. Lastly the entry costs have crazly skyrocket in last 10 years with little if no sense.

text_clean: I understand how Machu Picchu could once have been named one of the seven wonders of the modern world upon its discovery. A sacred place, suspended in the mountains and untouched by time — it must have felt like magic. But that time is long gone. Today, it’s overwhelmed by mass tourism. What truly broke it for me was seeing tourists cross safety barriers just to laugh and take selfies, ignoring the guards like misbehaving children. It made me question why I came at all. A place like this should be empty, inaccessible, preserved. I won’t return. I honestly hope it’s one day closed to protect its beauty and the peace it deserves. But in a capitalist world with millions of people all following the same paths — myself included — that probably won’t happen any time soon

text_clean: All in all, what you see from the outside is what you get. As Canadians visiting in February, we made the grave error of visiting during the heat of the day. This place is a hot hellscape on a Peruvian summer day. Only accessible during a tour, the location offers no respite from the sun. The tour takes a full hour, which, while very interesting and educational, is a bit of a long time to be looking at bricks. text_clean: El lugar histórico bonito. Se aprecia la cultura del pasado. Es muy concurrido por los turistas, el sitio es seguro. Una de las cosas malas es que nunca hay donde estacionar ... Tienes que parquear a cuadras del lugar. Pero bueno igual no pasa nada. Y lo otro es que hay un trabajador de alli algo mal educado, que cree saberlo todo, un poco antipático... Pero con ponerlo en su sitio basta. Es lamentable que tengan personal no apto para atender a los turistas y público en general. . . De igual forma. Mi recomendación es que vayan a visitar el lugar. Les encantará.

text_clean: No existe comunicación entre el teleferico y Kuelap, a pesar de ser la única opción para llegar (sino debes caminar más de 4 hrs). No compren las entradas por adelantado, si te pasas de la hora no te dejan entrar y si hay algún problema no te cambian la entrada. En el teleferico hay un pequeño stand y ahi puedes comprarlas (solo por internet!) Es un bello lugar pero la administración deja mucho que desear, solo puedes hacer 1 recorrido, así que hazlo lento porque no te permiten retroceder en el camino. Me voy con la sensación de que solo les interesa el dinero y no se preocupan del turista, ni de la experiencia vivida.

text_clean: Pretty nice and interesting historical place, if a little overhyped. Our experience was dampened by a terrible guide who repeated the same 3-4 facts that could be found in any guidebook over the course of 45mins. Afterwards we went round again and there was enough information available on the walls and the leaflet you get given with entry (unlike in several other attractions in Peru) to enjoy a slower self guided tour. That being said, we heard a couple of more diligent guides while on our second lap so perhaps we were just unlucky.

text_clean: So disappointed to have made a 40 minute journey across town to see Huaca Pucilana after reading about the site in an official leaflet, only to be told that you need a prior appointment that wasn't advertised on the leaflet I had. Upon showing the leaflet to the attendant, I'd have thought they would have showed a little more consideration given the leaflet was one of their own. Unfortunately there are times when common sense is outweighed by someone wearing a uniform, and they'd much prefer to sit around twiddling their thumbs. A walk around the site enabled us to get an idea of the sheer scale of the place, and to witness a guide showing only 2 people around. Really busy. To see their llamas caged in to such a small area when there's so much space for them to roam is a complete mystery to me. Disgraceful mistreatment of animals.

text_clean: Kuélap is absolutely amazing! The place is full of energy and history, surrounded by stunning vegetation and mountain views. The whole tour inside takes about 2 hours, with lots of ruins and impressive walls to explore. Entrance is S/ 20 (online only). To get there you have three options: • Hiking (very hard), • By car (Nogalcucho or Choctamal), • Or the cable car, which we chose and highly recommend! It costs around S/ 27 per person, takes about 25 minutes, and offers breathtaking views. Important note: Kuélap is not only a fortress, but a whole fortified city built by the Chachapoyas culture around 900–1100 AD. The cable car itself was built with French technology and is super modern and safe. Definitely one of the most impressive experiences in northern Peru!

text_clean: Me encanto la vibra y la paz que se siente en este lugar, es increíble. Hace honor a su nombramiento como Maravilla del mundo. Yo fui con una agencia lo cual me facilitó todo, me esperaba un guía en cada momento para acompañar todo mi recorrido. text_clean: I visited the archaeological site today, and although the tour was interesting, I was shocked by the living standards of the animals on the site, present to illustrate the resources of the ancient people. There were ducks with no access to fresh water, which is part of their essential living environment - instead they had a stagnant, filthy puddle and were surrounded by their own waste. The guinea pigs were also living in tiny pens, covered in their own waste. I would ask the site management to please rethink the provisions they are giving to these animals - perhaps even charging a slightly higher ticket price to allow for a better environment. The current living standards are really not appropriate, and lets down an otherwise good value tour.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo en cuanto a objetos y diseño es un 10/10. PERO llegué 430pm, 1era vez que visito el museo, cuando me vendieron las entradas, nunca me dijeron q la hora de cierre estaba próxima. Después cuando estaba dentro disfrutando del museo, alguien del museo me apuró, me dijo "en 1 hr se ve todo, ajustando 45min". Ir a un museo es una experiencia, sobre todo un museo con tantos detalles como este. No pude leer ni admirar el 50% de los objetos. Cada objeto tiene un texto bastante detallado además de ser obras muy bien trabajadas, obras q uno necesita procesar con tiempo, admirar. Soy de Lima, no sé cuando regrese, lamentablemente no tuve una buena experiencia. Sí antes de venderme la entrada me hubieran dicho "señor estamos próximos a cerrar, tome en cuenta q es probable que el tiempo no le sea suficiente" ya sabría a lo q me estaba lanzando. Sí van a ir, calculen 2 hrs para admirar todo, para procesar el arte de cada obra y no ir al museo solo por entrar y ya.

text_clean: Fascinating study of the dedication of the ancients in building structures covering great areas with handmade vertical bricks. On some you can even see their hand and finger prints! Our guide shared a wide scope of knowledge from all of those who lived here, their customs, foods they planted and consumed, burial rites, beliefs, skills... He was a great guide to his heritage and this enormous site in the middle of the city. (Unfortunately, there is a listing for a "minizoo" here. While seeing the 7 famous llamas, alpacas and dozens+ guinea pigs was an interesting part of the tour...that does Not qualify the Very minimum pen and feeding bin as a "zoo." There is a genuine one elsewhere. The handful of indigenousanimals here are just to enlighten visitors as to the animals the original denizens had for food, like the well-planted garden.) This is a healthy, enlightening educational walk through an amazing ancient world. (PS- Please bring some currency to show your appreciation to your fascinating guide!!)

text_clean: In September 1926 , Professor Julio C.Tello and his team of archaeologists were digging in the heart of the Nazca Valley in Peru . One day , two of the team members went for a walk . They wandered off in the direction of a hill . When they reached the top , they looked down and noticed something truly spectacular . The desert below was full of huge drawings of various geometric shapes . Apar from those there were also drawings of animals , birds , insects . Some were about two hundred meters long . Scientific testing confirmed that the drawings were more than two thousand years old . Scientists have many theories about why the ancient Nazca people made these lines . However ,for the time being , there is no definite answer . Some believe that the Nazca people knew how to fly and could see the drawings from the sky . Others believe they were a special calendar for the stars . Nowadays ,the Nazca lines are one of Peru 's most famous landmarks and if you want to experience them for yourself , you can go on a guided tour .

Pueden ahorrar se el dinero del bus si caminan a machupichu, es muy lindo y con tiempo suben en unas 1 hora y media y la verdad es lo mejor de ir a machupichu. La dificultad no es alta pese a unos escalones. 3. Compren circuito 2. 4. Los guías son súper groseros sino les compras su tour, somos colombianos y cuando le dijimos a una guía que no queríamos el servicio nos dijo pobres. 5. Es linda la vista pero la logística es muy mala, la gente de los tours se atiborra 6. Lleven gorra, gafas, filtro solar. 7. Compren los tiquetes del ciurcuito un día antes antes de la entrada a machupichu, comprarlo meses antes por la página no es necesario, siempre hay tiquetes (si vas a hacer esto quédate un día antes porque solo así los venden)
label: neutro

text_clean: As far as most historic centers go in Latin American metropolisis, this is definitely one that has been taken care and preserved by the government and it's citizenry. It's historical buildings and facades showcase the architecture that was prevalent during the colonial era and it's promenades, plazas, government palaces and Catholic places of worship are grand buildings worthy of preservation and conservation efforts for generations to come. It's beautiful but like all great modern cosmopolitan cities, Limas historic center also has pickpockets and people of ill-will looking out for bedazzled tourists sporting their wealth through their jewelry, flimsy backpacks, and expensive camera equipment. This place is worth the visit but just be extremely aware of your surroundings at all times and wary of "friendly" strangers who approach you attempting to make casual conversation. In particular, stay away from the young, urban men selling "Tattoo" services, which after a while becomes almost amusing, as if it was the only clandestine service in need of a large throng of roaming salesmen throughout the plazas and public walkways of this grand historical center. Please visit and enjoy during the day but be extremely careful in the late afternoon hours and postpone coming at night, unless accompanied by tour guides or citizens of Lima aware of what's going on. Many of these warnings are common sense, applicable to all major Latin American historical centers but people lower their guards when they become enchanted taking pictures or gazing upon the beautiful buildings. To it's credit, the government is aware of it's issue and has deployed a good show of force with it's tourism police officers who walk throughout the center wiling to offer assistance. Great place to take pictures, but never let your guard down.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: This is a cool historical site surrounded by sea of police corruption. We were there in December and five (5) police shakedowns later, my son and I made it from the Nazca desert back to Lima. Our strategies included dividing up the $100 dollars the Hilton gave us on credit (after the police stole all of our money a day earlier). We divided the $ and hid it in various locations in the car. Each time I would reload my wallet with only $20 dollars. Each time the Peru Police pulled us over and demanded money for not having a fire extinguisher in the car, for having the lights on, for NOT having the lights on...they just pulled out their little book of violations and pointed to a violation and demanded payment on the spot. Each time my wallet only had $20 and we said that was all we had and we would be happy to pay by credit card (which of course they could not take...). We kept our original passports hidden and just used the copies of our documents that the Hilton made for us. We would say that "the police already kept our original documents and we don't have them anymore and are going to the American Consulate in Lima to ask for them to be replaced." One group of police actually let us go with no payment at all...so that was positive.... I've been to 84 countries. This is the most corrupt place that I have ever seen. Sometimes the traffic fines come with death threats. Seeing the lines by aircraft was interesting but not worth the price of all the police terrorism to get down there from Lima.

Taking the bus up the steep slope along a narrow road of switchbacks and no guardrail was definitely an experience I won’t forget.

text_clean: We stayed at the Aranwa Resorts, and Spa, Paracas, Peru for two nights in May, 2018. The suite given to us was quite spacious and had a balcony overlooking the Paracas Bay. Even though the hotel is located on a sprawling area, there was no coffee maker or kettle in the room, the refrigerator was missing even though there was space in the modular unit in the suite. There was no running hot water in the room on the second day of our stay. We informed the front desk of the issue, but they did not rectify the problem satisfactory to us. Also no one speaks even simple English and communication with the hotel staff was a major issue. Based on the above we are giving a 3 star rating inspite of the resort having fantastic facilities.

text_clean: Stunning place - but for the life of us we could not work out what the 70 soles per person fee was going to. The whole thing stank of corruption and lining local officials pockets - we were told it was for tourism infrastructure, but this was few and far between. And as a tourist I'm already paying for my hotel, transport and food that is going to benefit the local economy, I'm not completely sure what extra this was going to - just a payment to look at nature? Yes, the roads were good, but I'm pretty sure that's more because of the plethora of mining companies in the area. Seemed just like seeing foreign tourists as open wallets and was definitely not a great side to see of Peru.

And as a tourist I'm already paying for my hotel, transport and food that is going to benefit the local economy, I'm not completely sure what extra this was going to - just a payment to look at nature? Yes, the roads were good, but I'm pretty sure that's more because of the plethora of mining companies in the area. Seemed just like seeing foreign tourists as open wallets and was definitely not a great side to see of Peru.
label: neutro

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Nazca Lines is a great art work on the very large Canvas of Earth, carved out by excavating the soil of the Nazca Desert in southern Peru.They were created between 500 BC and 500 AD. No modern technology no modern Canvas at that time. These large geoglyphs are indeed wonderful world of art . Mirror to advancement of human creativity. Most lines run straight across the landscape. There are also depiction of animals and plants, made up of lines. The combined length of all the lines is over 1,300 km. It is preserved till today naturally due to its isolation and the dry, windless, stable climate of the plateau. But anthropogenic interference may obliterate it's beauty in coming years. In 1994 this has been declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Great salute to the artists selecting the Nature's Canvas which stood the test of time for the last 2500 years. Good luck.

text_clean: I paid 100 PEN with an agency to get here which included transportation, meal, and entry. I think you need to buy entry tickets in advance. There were a couple small areas on each end that were closed, but you can still see everything. Pictures are less picturesque because there is a tin roof over the entryway walls. I consider it “open” and wouldn’t be deterred by the reviews saying it’s closed or partially closed. I think the biggest difference between Machu Pichu and here is that Machu Pichu is in a saddle so you can climb a mountain for those famous pictures. Kuelap is the top of the mountain so you can’t photograph the whole city. My tour was in Spanish, so I didn’t understand everything, but the views and experience were good. The walls are meant to hold in the dirt. The Chachapoya essentially created some level space to build on and it’s not considered a fort it’s a religious site. Lastly, the gondola (cabinas) station is marked permanently closed, but it’s actually open and located where it’s supposed to be on the map. You board a bus there and they take you further up the mountain to get onto the gondola.

Los guías son muy buenos, el paseo tiene una duración de alrededor de hora y media o dos. Es recomendable llevar sombrero por el sol y buen calzado. La arena y el reflejo del lugar pueden jugar una mala pasada si no se lleva ropa apropiada. De todas formas, si uno se cansa, puede pedir al guía y éste le indica por dónde abandonar el recorrido. Hay un restaurante con platos típicos muy bueno dentro del predio. Si se busca algo más económico alrededor hay buenas opciones.

Los objetos no son suficientes como para entenderlo todo. Creo que ese es su problema, descansan en la arquitectura que es preciosa y ya. La entrada es muy cara, 40 soles, no incluye visita guiada, para tener una debes pagar 20 soles más. La relación precio/servicio no es buena. Es una pena porque el lugar es hermoso, pero no te llevas nada de información, solo fotos bonitas.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Is undoubtedly one of the most incredible places we've ever been too, helped by good weather and a quieter day with fewer people. However, there's a few things though that could be done to improve the experience, particularly given the hefty price tag attached to the visit. - There is zero information around the site about it's history or design. When visiting one of the wonders of the world it seems insane that zero information is provided to let people learn more about the sites rich past. I presume this is to encourage the use of guides, but regardless a small number of boards, even just indicating what certain spaces are or what they were used for, should be a minimum. - Toilets should be free. Given the amount it costs, there is no excuse for charging for the toilets at the top of the hill. If required, get people to show their tickets before being able to use the bathroom, but to outright charge is just a desperate attempt to squeeze you for a couple more dollars

text_clean: As a native English speaker, I greatly appreciated English on some of the exhibits. You cannot expect English in all of them, and you can't bring your phone in, so be prepared to read some Spanish info without access to Google Translate. Even if you speak no Spanish at all, this museum is a must-see if you ever find yourself with some spare time in the Chiclayo area. They have some of the most spectacular jewlery that I have ever seen in a museum in Peru, the kind of stuff that ancient royalty used to wear, kept in perfect condition

text_clean: Don’t do the Nazca Line flight, it’s expensive and not that impressive. The tour costed $79 per person and when we get to the airport we still have to pay another 77 soles(about $21)for airport tax. The flight itself is horrible especially if you have motion sickness because the plane is small and very shaky. The pilot will just tell you to look left and right to look at the lines. Make you even more dizzy. The Nazca Line, in my opinion, doesn’t look like something ancient.

The flight itself is horrible especially if you have motion sickness because the plane is small and very shaky. The pilot will just tell you to look left and right to look at the lines. Make you even more dizzy. The Nazca Line, in my opinion, doesn’t look like something ancient.
label: neutro

text_clean: Machu Picchu is a tourist trap, I don’t recommend going there. You pay for entrance ticket, train, bus, toilets, everything. And have to wait in lines in every point for so long. The trip from Cusco and back took 16 hours and more than 200 dollars for what??? Only 1 hour walk through the route. The ruins aren’t impressive because they are not original but rebuilt. Go to other ruins around Cusco instead. And the last recommendation: do not be caught on trap, treat locals as they treat you. Don’t overpay, bargain when possible, don’t leave tips because you were already changed twice, bring your own food and drinks to have a lunch.

text_clean: El sitio es increíble, le doy un 2 por la penosa organización que hay y el dinero que te dejas para poder verlo, un completo sin sentido. El primer punto negativo es tener que elegir que parte del lugar ver me parece un sinsentido, nosotros pagábamos por el tour 3A con Huaynapichu que no es especialmente económico y nos fuimos con la sensación de que nos faltaba por ver una gran parte. Además de que tienes el tiempo cobrado, en nuestro caso solo podíamos estar dentro 2h. Hay que añadir que antes de poder entrar hay que esperar una hora en el pueblo para que te suba el autobús, y después otros cuarenta minutos para entrar. Nuestra entrada era a las 7.00, terminamos entrando a las 7.30. El tour y la visita a la montaña fue bien, pero al salir estuvimos otra hora esperando hasta montar en el autobús de vuelta al pueblo. No me quiero imaginar estas esperas con frío o lluvia, pues estás completamente a la intemperie. Es algo que la autoridad competente tiene que pensar, no pueden tener a la gente en estas condiciones. En la vida habíamos visto algo así.

text_clean: Historia del Perú, parte de ella resumida en este magnífico lugar. Lo que te enseñaron en el cole lo encuentras ahí y vivirás en carne propia la experiencia. Eso sí, no se permite el acceso de cámaras al Inter

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: The Royal Tombs of Sipán Museum near Chiclayo is a must-visit if you love history and archaeology. The pyramid-style architecture itself reflects the ancient Moche culture, and inside you walk through the story of the Lord of Sipán step by step, from excavation to the stunning treasures found in his tomb. The gold, silver, ceramics, and textiles give a deep insight into the artistry and beliefs of the Mochica civilization. The museum is very well maintained — clean, organized, and carefully curated with proper lighting and security that protect these priceless artifacts. The exhibits are presented with respect and detail, making it easy to appreciate the importance of this culture in Peru’s history. Some explanations are only in Spanish and weekends can be a bit crowded, but overall it is one of the best museums in Peru and highly recommended 😊😊😊

text_clean: I recently visited Machu Picchu with my partner, and while the site itself is undeniably breathtaking, I am deeply concerned about the lack of proper emergency response systems in place for visitors experiencing medical emergencies. During our visit, my partner, as he got out of the bus, slipped because of the uneven surface and slippery as the road was wet, he injured his ankle. He suffered a concerning medical episode— things unfolded very quickly - he was in a lot of pain, he collapsed and experienced convulsions - due to what we believe was a combination of altitude, and intense pain of the injury. In such a high-traffic and physically demanding site, we expected a prompt and trained medical response. Unfortunately, that was not the case. There was no visible or accessible medical staff nearby, and it took far too long to get any form of assistance. The staff present seemed untrained to handle emergencies such as this and there was no coordinated protocol to help us manage the situation. Thankfully my partner recovered but that was a very stressful moment for us. I am writing this not in anger, but in hopes that the management will: Review and strengthen their emergency protocols. Ensure that trained medical staff are stationed on-site in different posts at all times Install clearly marked emergency points or communication stations Train staff in basic first aid and response procedures Machu Picchu is one of the most visited tourist destinations in the world. With the number of visitors—including elderly people, children, and those not acclimated to the altitude—accidents and health emergencies are not rare, and a more robust system must be put in place to prevent avoidable harm. I truly hope the management takes this seriously. The experience itself is magical, but basic safety and human response must be part of that experience.

text_clean: I recently visited Machu Picchu with my partner, and while the site itself is undeniably breathtaking, I am deeply concerned about the lack of proper emergency response systems in place for visitors experiencing medical emergencies. During our visit, my partner, as he got out of the bus, slipped because of the uneven surface and slippery as the road was wet, he injured his ankle. He suffered a concerning medical episode— things unfolded very quickly - he was in a lot of pain, he collapsed and experienced convulsions - due to what we believe was a combination of altitude, and intense pain of the injury. In such a high-traffic and physically demanding site, we expected a prompt and trained medical response. Unfortunately, that was not the case. There was no visible or accessible medical staff nearby, and it took far too long to get any form of assistance. The staff present seemed untrained to handle emergencies such as this and there was no coordinated protocol to help us manage the situation. text_clean: Para ir primero, toman carro en la terminal hacia kuelap que está 7 soles. Luego toman el teleferico que está 20,40 soles por persona que incluye ida y vuelta en la couster y teleférico 🚡 y que solo lo pueden usar hasta la hora que te indican, sino deberás volver a pagar los 20,40 soles para regresar. Al llegar deben pagar 30 soles por persona adulta para ingresar a kuelap, y finalmente subir a kuelap que es 30 minutos de caminata hacia arriba. De bajada es más rápido. A mi opinión personal, pienso que está sobrevalorado el costo de ingresar, ya que hay miradores con árboles de eucalipto y frutos en el camino, donde la entrada es 3 soles y que están cerca, asimismo podrían ir a las cataratas de gocta más temprano o los miradores de huancas y uno más que no me acuerdo. La verdad, que más me emocionó subir al teleferico 🚡 que llegar a kuelap, que fue la única decepción de mi viaje, además que ya no permiten ingresar a las casitas para tomar foto. Antes se podía según lo que me contó una persona que ya visitaba el lugar por segunda vez. Olvidaba, para ir a kuelap NO TOMEN GUIADO NI TOUR. no es necesario, es fácil de llegar.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: The content of the museum is amazing and some of the best I've seen in South America. Unfortunately the museum is massively let down by the lack of story and content conveyed by the information boards. Also none of the information on the second and third, the lowest level, has any translation into other languages and the top level only into English (about 25% of the whole museum) so its pointless going if you want to learn and not just see pretty objects. I believe they have the option of a guide to squeeze more money from you if you like. I'd recommend reading up beforehand so you know what your going to see.

text_clean: Para ir al machu picchu tienes que comprar las entradas con 2 o 3 meses de antelacion. He ido con mi familia y no es lo que esperaba, tienes que pagar por todo, si vas a subir a las montañas tienes que ir preparado con mucha agua y ropa comoda. Además para entrar al machu picchu tienes que pagar por circuitos, antes no hacia falta ya que con el circuito 1 se podia ver todo, ahora tienes que pagar por ver cada cosa, por ejemplo: en el circuito 1 solo ves el machu picchu desde fuera y subir la montaña, nada más. Si subes las montañas es bajo tu responsabilidad y si te caes o te haces daño tienes que pagar mucho dinero para un medico (creo que mas de 100 dolares). Tampoco es que al subir la montaña haiga una recompensa tremenda, subi la montaña del circuito 1 y solo habian unas sillas y un letrero de cuanta altura tiene la montaña. Puedes sacar buenas fotos pero nada más, si no traes agua, mejor ni lo intentes, tienes tiempo limite hasta las 12:00 mediodia para subir y se tarda unas 4 horas a paso lento. Si vas a subir tambien recomiendo llevar repelentes para los mosquitos ya que hay muchos mosquitos. Si te gusta escalar este viaje es para ti, pero si eres un poco sedentario no vale la pena pagar tanto solo para subir una montaña y tomarte unas cuantas fotos, por cierto, si llueve olvidate de subir los cerros, es imposible y probablemente te rompas o te hagas daño o peor. No hay seguridad alguna. Sinceramente es bonito para pasar el dia pero tienes que comprar los demas circuitos (1,2,3,4) , porque si no, no puedes ver nada. Recalco otra vez, tienes que pagar por todo, TODO. Las fotos son preciosas pero no todo lo que brilla es oro. Se podria distribuir mejor y que todo no sea tan caro. Igualmente muy bonito Perú y hermoso el machu picchu aunque con lo que cobran se podria mejor muchisimo.

text_clean: Hoy 01/feb/2026 totalmente descepcionada del servicio en este parque mágico del agua. Antes yo venía con mis hijos y podíamos sentarnos a relajarnos en el gras. Si tienes hijos ven con minino 100 soles para los juegos comida aparte y si no tienes hijos hay lugares más bonitos en dónde pasear en pareja. No lo vale en serio no gasten ni su tiempo ni su dinero.

text_clean: Muy bonito el lugar, la entrada son 15 soles, si tienes carnet de estudiante o docente de cualquier parte del mundo te hacen 50% de descuento en la entrada. Siempre vas acompañado de un guía y los recorridos son cada hora, con una duración aprox de 1 hora, te llevan por toda la huaca contando la historia y al final te llevan a la zona donde tienen la vegetación y animales que consumían en esa época, ahí podrás conocer a las llamas. El lugar en si es muy intagrameable, vale totalmente la pena, no olviden de preferencia ir con ropa cubierta por el sol y usar mucho bloqueador o en su defecto llevar sombrilla. Al final antes de salir puedes entrar a un museo donde verás objetos reales de la época y su explicación. El horario es hasta las 4:30 p.m. así que estar muy pendiente para no perder la ida

text_clean: A spectacular canyon, and in my view even more spectacular historical site. The terraces built by the pre-inca civilitation that stretch through the valley are stunning. I cannot begin to imagine the amount of labour and sheer effort that went into building these. There are some excellent opportunities to take short hikes to scenic viewpoints, and inbetween you get around easily by bus or car, so it's very well suited for the not so outdoor-minded among us, which includes me. There is also an active volcanoe that erupts from time to time, making for a spectacular view.

text_clean: Lo que menos nos gustó de nuestro viaje a Perú. Las líneas en sí son interesantes, pero todo lo que rodea a la gestión deja mucho que desear. Es muy caro para lo que ves, más el pago de 2 tasas diferentes una vez allí. Además, el vuelo en la avioneta dura media hora y las explicaciones son prácticamente inexistentes más allá de "aquí tenemos la figura del mono" En esa línea. Si pillara más de paso... Bueno. Pero pilla muy a desmano para solo media hora de sobrevuelo y ninguna explicación. Nos explicaron mil veces más cosas en Paracas sobre la única figura del candelabro que se encuentra allí, que en todas estas figuras en Nazca.

text_clean: I went here on recommendation. I was not disappointed. The restaurant is large with outside/patio seating as well, with the ruins as the backdrop. Great for large groups. The service was great. I think they serviced the best Pisco sour I had in Lima. The Guinea pig I had here was a fillet version and definitely allowed me to appreciate the taste of these local delicacies (I had alpaca as well). Overall the taste and quality of the meals and experience here was outstanding. Highly recommend both the museum tour and restaurant.

text_clean: Como siempre Macchu Picchu es el mejor lugar para recargar baterías y llenarse de energías. Por la Pandemia hay pocos turistas y se puede caminar tranquilamente y tomar muchas fotos con calma. Se cumplen todas las medidas de bioseguridad, es obligatorio el uso de mascarilla y protector facial en el bus y durante la caminata se debe tener la mascarilla en todo momento. Recomiendo pasar una noche en Aguas Calientes, ahora los hoteles tienen muchas ofertas, yo me hospedé en el Inti Punku Macchu Picchu Suites y el servicio fue estupendo, muy limpio, con habitaciones amplias, algunas con vista a la montaña e incluye el desayuno buffet. Si se quedan una noche después de ir a MP pueden hacer caminata a los Jardines de Mandor ..es un parque botánico silvestre que de encuentra a 1 hora más o menos ( depende del estado físico) el final del recorrido tiene una cascada increíble ( entrada 10 soles solo efectivo) . En el hotel les indican como llegar. Y para almorzar el restaurante Full House con vista al río, la comida es deliciosa y la atención de Cristian fue insuperable. Una experiencia maravillosa!!!

input_modelo: aspecto: alojamiento reseña: Como siempre Macchu Picchu es el mejor lugar para recargar baterías y llenarse de energías. Por la Pandemia hay pocos turistas y se puede caminar tranquilamente y tomar muchas fotos con calma. Se cumplen todas las medidas de bioseguridad, es obligatorio el uso de mascarilla y protector facial en el bus y durante la caminata se debe tener la mascarilla en todo momento. Recomiendo pasar una noche en Aguas Calientes, ahora los hoteles tienen muchas ofertas, yo me hospedé en el Inti Punku Macchu Picchu Suites y el servicio fue estupendo, muy limpio, con habitaciones amplias, algunas con vista a la montaña e incluye el desayuno buffet. Si se quedan una noche después de ir a MP pueden hacer caminata a los Jardines de Mandor ..es un parque botánico silvestre que de encuentra a 1 hora más o menos ( depende del estado físico) el final del recorrido tiene una cascada increíble ( entrada 10 soles solo efectivo) . En el hotel les indican como llegar. Y para almorzar el restaurante Full House con vista al río, la comida es deliciosa y la atención de Cristian fue insuperable. Una experiencia maravillosa!!!
label: neutro

text_clean: As of a few weeks ago because they’re busy making repairs after a colapse, it opened back up and we were allowed to view the outside of one of the entrances, but not go inside…. yet I found it worth it for the journey up that demonstrated how steep and inaccessible the place is to get to being 10,000 feet up. After seeing the ruins and taking that 1 kilometer hike up to narrow entrance (one of three), I enjoyed how I could now notice bits of ruins and the edge of the fortress peeking out from above on my way down while in the cable car to try to imagine what the Inca saw when going up and taking over the Cloud people… it surely deserves five stars when fully open. … but still worth the money and worth exploring north Peru

text_clean: Visitar Machu Picchu fue una experiencia inolvidable. La energía del lugar es única, y cada rincón cuenta una historia que trasciende el tiempo. La majestuosidad de las montañas, el silencio sagrado que se respira y la perfección de las construcciones incas te dejan sin palabras. Recomiendo ir con guía para entender mejor la historia y el significado de cada espacio. Es un destino que emociona, que conecta con algo profundo y que todo el mundo debería conocer al menos una vez en la vida. ¡Una maravilla del mundo que realmente hace honor a su título! Recomiendo entrar en el primer turno así el sol no está tan fuerte. Yo entré con guías de Perú Magic y fueron increíbles, mejoraron la experiencia.

text_clean: Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The ticket prices are quite high, yet the trains are old and unreliable. Our departure was delayed by an hour because the train broke down en route. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

Then, onsite, they don't accept credit card to buy the télécabin tickets, And The First Atm is 1h of car away!!! You​ go to the fortress and you find a macchu picchu-like circuit were tourists are parked like cattle, even though they are very few. A lot of people fake-working. No shelter for the rain for the tourists (only for the workers). Etcetc You can do better Peru. This is the worst place I've visited here. The Amazonas has way better spots.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo está cerrado y los restos reales de la tumba de Sipán no se pueden visitar. Según el personal, hay problemas con el aire acondicionado y tardarán un año en volver a abrir completamente al público. Se pueden visitar tres espacios con réplicas y algunos hallazgos interesantes, pero si vienes a Lambayeque a ver al “Tutankamon peruano” te irás de aquí sin verlo. Es una lástima que el Ministerio de Cultura haya dejado un espacio como este en condiciones tan precarias y que no facilite la información con antelación. Me voy decepcionado y esperando que en el futuro se tomen en serio la protección y muestra de la rica cultura arqueológica de Lambayeque.

Se pueden visitar tres espacios con réplicas y algunos hallazgos interesantes, pero si vienes a Lambayeque a ver al “Tutankamon peruano” te irás de aquí sin verlo. Es una lástima que el Ministerio de Cultura haya dejado un espacio como este en condiciones tan precarias y que no facilite la información con antelación. Me voy decepcionado y esperando que en el futuro se tomen en serio la protección y muestra de la rica cultura arqueológica de Lambayeque.

text_clean: El lugar y su historia así como también todo lo que han rescatado y recuperado es impresionante. Es una pena que tengan animales en cautiverio por el solo hecho de representar lo que hacían las comunidades indígenas en su entonces. Los cobayos se lastiman entre sí, las alpacas viven en espacios tan reducidos que no tienen pasto natural ni sombra de un árbol solo un techo de metal. Es lamentable que esto ocurra en el siglo XXI, pudieron recrearlo con maquetas o filmaciones sin necesidad de tener los animales en ese estado. Fue muy decepcionante.

text_clean: I know, I know.. you’d think I’d give a place like Machu Picchu more than 3 stars… but honestly I’ve been here twice and, while definitely dramatic looking, unless you seriously love Incan culture or are willing to pay for a guide, you won’t get much out of the experience other than Instagram content… Speaking of which, if you want to avoid getting a million people in your photos you have GOT to pick the first slots of the morning (06:00a), I can’t stress this enough… don’t go after 10am unless you lovvvvvve huge crowds, heat, and long queues. The Peruvian government is keeping some of the silly covid changes in place, namely the unidirectional walking path and new “circuits” method of ticketing where they’ve split the park up and you have to purchase multiple tickets to see the whole thing, an obvious money grab at this point and another reason I knocked off a star.

text_clean: It's not a big deal. It's the main square because of its political importance. The buildings are pretty and important, but you can't visit most of them. There are literally dozens of sellers trying to sell you all sorts of things, from coca leaves to umbrellas on a sunny day, specially if you are a tourist. Besides, now (Feb, 2018) Peru is going through a moment of political instability, so it's very likely to witness a bunch of ongoing protests at any giving time you visit the square. I actually like to watch those social movements, but that's a personal matter. Some may get a little bit scared by the powerful presence of police calvary.

text_clean: I’m giving a 1-star review for the absolutely terrible new ticketing system. Forcing visitors to see only one tiny section of Machu Picchu is a horrendous idea, and if other world wonders followed this model, world tourism would collapse. My experience was a complete disaster. I originally had a ticket for Circuit No. 2 and wanted to go to the top, but my official tour guide insisted the top was part of Circuit No. 1. So I purchased an additional ticket for Circuit No. 1 just to make sure I could see everything. Then, when I tried to go up, the staff told me that what I wanted to see was not Ticket No. 1 but it was actually Circuit No. 2, and because my original Circuit No. 2 ticket had been valid in the morning, it was now “expired”. So despite buying different tickets, they still refused to let me see the top of circuit 2 from Machu Picchu. Their only response was to tell me to complain to my tour guide, which is absolutely useless and does nothing to fix the fact that I ended up out of one important part I came to see. What makes this even worse is their complete lack of empathy. They don’t understand, or probably don’t care, that people wait months or even years to visit Machu Picchu, and for many it’s a once-in-a-lifetime experience. If someone has a VALID ticket, even if it expired, DURING LOW SEASONS, a few hours earlier due to confusing instructions, the staff should show some humanity and let them in. There is absolutely no reason to be so strict. It was even late afternoon and there wasn't any crowd at all. Instead, the rangers and gate agents were rude, unhelpful, inflexible, and completely indifferent. They just recite the same memorized line "your ticket expired" and refuse to help in any real way. Honestly, AI robots would do a far better job than the staff currently working there. I had heard many complaints about Machu Picchu, but now I've experienced this myself. Hope this review helps someone

text_clean: Al atardecer, llegando la noche, las luces empiezan a dar color y movimiento a las distintas piletas de agua, entre clásicas, túneles o chorros de diferente altura. El público toma ubicación en las distintas sillas ubicadas frente a la pileta central y empieza el espectáculo de luces y colores, entonces el parque se convierte en perfecta sincronía con la noche. Puedes ingresar después de adquirir tu entrada, visitantes nacionales o niños pagan 4 soles. Llegar antes de las 6pm te permite apreciar las distintas áreas verdes y monumentos. Interesante propuesta en Lima, para escapar de la agitación citadina.

text_clean: For me, it's an Overrated marketing success of an abandoned settlement. I will submit that the majestic mountains and valleys are the main draw and attraction. The hyped settlement architecture? It is just a cluster of abandoned stone/rock/mud low 'buildings' meant for 3-400 families. Sure, some walls are tightly stacked with big chipped/carved boulders/rocks and even a blade can't get thru the gaps... The mountain terrace carved/landscaped for agriculture is not unique to the Inca. The use of gravity to channel water is common amongst other old civilisations too... With a good guide, the story telling of Inca civilisation and the Inca connection to mother earth and nature can be engaging. Know too that getting there to MP is a long journey and time wasting pain, compounded with an eventual convoluted Q system to get expensive tickets. (It can be a 2 day Q for a 3hrs just one section visit of MP). .. And a tourist trap village venue to take a bus up to MP. When there, Also beware the nasty midges/sand flies which can carry the deadly oropouch virus. Only this is free. 🤣😂 Oh, the story of the hacked down obelisk to accommodate the Prez helicopter landing is outlandish.

text_clean: Ayer fui al circuito mágico del agua. Una pésima idea, fui con un adulto mayor y niños, no hay asientos disponibles y en una calor extrema, no hay donde taparse o asombrarse del del sol cuando quisimos sentarnos en el en la zonas verdes nos dijeron que estaba prohibido y tuvimos que estar parados cerca de las tres horas que estuvimos con el adulto mayor y no pudimos sentarnos a ningún lugar ya que todo estaba repleto; pero tengo que hacer una observación. Hay muchos juegos para niños que están posicionados en muchas áreas verdes,mientras a la gente que paga una entrada, no se nos permitía ni siquiera sentarnos dentro de estas zonas ya que no hay asientos suficiente, me parece muy pésima idea es un sitio donde uno quiere ir a a observar las aguas, las piletas, etc. pero lo han convertido en un sitio donde se está monetizando y muchas personas que venimos de fuera quedamos decepcionados , Prefiero ir a otros lugares donde te traten mejor. No les sugiero que vayan, tengan otras opciones para poder salir un fin de semana a divertirse acá en Lima.

text_clean: The guards act irresponsibly and put the guests' health at risk by taking away their water. Attached is a photo of the entrance where this happend. We did Circuit 3 and got through the entrance of circuit 3/4 without any problems. When we entered again (through the entrance to the Mountain, which is also for Circuit 1+2) our backpack was suddenly too big. We had (for 2 people) 3l of water in a drinking system built into the backpack, 2 breads in a foldable can, sunscreen and 2 rain jackets. So nothing big, no plastic waste and our backpack fit in the small model box. We discussed in Spanish whether they really wanted to take away our water for the strenuous hike up the mountain in the midday heat, but they remained adamant. This irresponsible behavior inevitably leads to dehydration, which is dangerous since there is no way to buy more water. Otherwise the purchased ticket will be useless. I am horrified by the conditions there and how carelessly they provoke medical emergencies.

text_clean: I wished I had read the review before going to this site. Since I am in Lima with local, I did not bother researching, which resulted in rather disappointing experience. When you walk in, you have to wait for a guide. You cannot roam alone. We were told the next tour will start in 20 min later when we entered the site. We waited close to 30 min when the tour started. I asked if there is an English tour, and the response was 20 min later. I wish there was a set time so we did not have to sit and wait doing nothing. I decided to take the Spanish tour with my limited Spanish. I read this place up in Wiki and a few other websites night before. It is a cool place and had a great view on the top. I could not understand the majority of things guide said, and I was roaming alone among the groups. When the tour was done, there were about 5 guides sitting around there and doing nothing. I was thinking where were they before? Maybe night tour is much more popular, and all of them were gearing towards the night tour. Overall, I was disappointed at the experience.

text_clean: Es posible visualizar tres de las figuras desde el mirador instalado a la vera de la carretera Panamericana. Sus líneas son de unos 20 cm de ancho y el tamaño de las imágenes es fácilmente visible desde baja altura. El mantenimiento del sitio es escaso, con basura acumulada, baños en estado higiénico terrible, y tejido plástico que perturban la vista de la figura que se encuentra atravesada por la carretera. It is possible to view three of the figures from the viewpoint installed on the side of the Pan-American Highway. Its lines are about 20 cm wide and the size of the images is easily visible from low height. The maintenance of the site is scarce, with accumulated garbage, bathrooms in terrible hygienic state, and plastic fabric that disturb the view of the figure that is crossed by the road.

text_clean: Amazing place, we went there at early hours so it wasn't very crowded. Although early its foggy so beware, sometimes it takes about an hour for it to clear out. We spent about 4hrs walking around checking the whole place out, walking at steady pace and stopping at every spot you're allowed and taking photos. Because of pandemic there are guards that will tell you to "catch up with your group" if they see you staying behind. They're just doing their jobs trying to keep us safe. 🤗 There is plenty of wildlife around, big and small so keep an eye open for those too if you are interested. If you are lucky you can spot Condors sometimes flying over, but difficult to capture with phone cameras because of the sun 😔

text_clean: Si vas a viajar a Arequipa, el monasterio es una parada que si o sí te lo recomiendo. El lugar es realmente enorme, puedes estar todo el tiempo que quieras, la historia es impresionante y es un vistazo a como era la vida de las mujeres que entraban al claustro. Excelente lugar para fotos, cada rincón del monasterio es hermoso. Recomiendo que tomen la opción de ir con una guía. Me toco Laura, una señora espectacular. Su guiado fue A1, su forma de contarnos la historia del monasterio refleja la pasión por la historia y el cariño que le tiene ese lugar. Definitivamente volvería a ir, vale la pena los 45 soles de entrada. Recomendación: vayan con zapatillas cómodas porque van a caminar mucho. Usen bloqueador. Y vayan acompañados de un buen fotógrafo 😂.

text_clean: Not handicapped friendly. I would love to tell you about this place but I opted not to tour. Reason was that all tours are guided. Handicapped people can only do 1/2 of the tour. The tour then leaves the handicapped person at a particular place in the very hot peruvian sun for however long the able bodied people take to finish the rest of the tour. We were told 30-45 minutes. Can you imagine putting your 85 year old grandmother old in the sun in the middle of the day for that long? I cannot and cannot relate to what the Peruvians are thinking!!! Obviously they are not. I consider that cruel and that I why i cannot comment except to say your handicapped policies suck!!!

text_clean: Pucllana Site Museum in Miraflores offers a fascinating glimpse into Lima’s pre-Incan history. The guided tours, included in the affordable entry fee, take you through the adobe pyramid, small museum exhibits, and a native flora and fauna area in about an hour. Its central location makes it easy to visit, and the contrast of ancient ruins amid the modern city is striking. While the site is historically significant, it’s relatively small and not as visually impressive as other Peruvian ruins. A worthwhile and educational stop if you’re nearby, but not a must-see if you’re short on time.

text_clean: Me parece indignante que tengan cuatro o cinco llamas, una alpaca y muchos cuyes hacinados en un espacio muy pequeño, en cautiverio solo para que los turistas los vean por unos segundos. Si me hubieran informado que habría animales, no habría entrado ni apoyado ese tipo de maltrato. Es completamente innecesario tener animales en esas condiciones. No lo recomiendo. En cuanto al guía y las ruinas, todo estuvo muy bien, pero mi experiencia quedó ensombrecida por el evidente maltrato animal. Recomiendo encarecidamente que reconsideren el uso de animales en estas condiciones y busquen alternativas más éticas para enriquecer la experiencia de los visitantes, sin recurrir al sufrimiento de seres vivos.

text_clean: Don’t bother. The site is closed and you can’t even walk along the base of the wall. You pretty much can get to a fence near where the entrance would have been. It sounds like it will be literally years at best before the site reopens. None of the guides or tour operators or people running the site want to admit that their ‘premiere site’ for northern Peru is closed to tourism for possibly a decade. I would vaguely guess they will need to build a roof over the entire structure at some point to keep stormwater from destabilizing the wall any further. Unless the want to tear sections of the wall down, put a drainage system behind the wall, and rebuild it with a modern support / drainage system behind the walls. Anyways this section of the country is fantastic, and you should totally visit it. But don’t pretend you are going to be able to walk around the top of Kuelap anytime this decade.

text_clean: Nos dió mucha rabia la entrada. Le dice a uno la guía ( Milagros) que uno tiene que reservar con un día de anticipación. Pero le ofrece al turista que si quiere si paga un monto de 10 soles extra ella hace el recorrido inmediatamente. Escuchando las conversaciones con los demás turistas reconocimos esto como una estafa. Pues Milagros y los demás trabajadores ofrecían esta "oportunidades" forma diferente. text_clean: What a waste of time! You go there and get tickets without a wait. The guide at the entrance who ended up being our guide during the tour told us the tour is mandatory and we have to tip her at the end. She repeated the tip part twice more during our tour but she was definitely bored and only interested in the money cause she would give us literally 1minute of basic info and then tell us to take photos while she walked on checking her phone for the entire hour! The info she gave us were marginally more than the info you read at the little room next to the ticket office and there was no explanation or contextualisation. Anyways, I felt it was a waste of time (the tour lasts one hour) and a shame she did not help us understand and appreciate more the ancient Lima culture. I was left confused because others have written amazing reviews so maybe it was just this lady guide that was really bad.

She repeated the tip part twice more during our tour but she was definitely bored and only interested in the money cause she would give us literally 1minute of basic info and then tell us to take photos while she walked on checking her phone for the entire hour! The info she gave us were marginally more than the info you read at the little room next to the ticket office and there was no explanation or contextualisation. Anyways, I felt it was a waste of time (the tour lasts one hour) and a shame she did not help us understand and appreciate more the ancient Lima culture. I was left confused because others have written amazing reviews so maybe it was just this lady guide that was really bad.
label: neutro

text_clean: Pasé una muy mala experiencia en la zona de ingreso a la maravilla. Soy una mujer indígena kichwa Otavaleña (Ecuador) y llegué usando mi ropa tradicional (nada extravagante ni fuera de lugar). La srta de ingreso me impedía entrar indicando que no podía ingresar vestida así, a pesar de indicarle lo anterior. Se portó antipática, nada amable. Me dijo que use una chompa para cubrir mi ropa (no llevé chompa ni nada más). También dijo algo que yo iba a hacer videos de publicidad y cosas parecidas... le dije que no era así y que si deseaba podía revisar mi celular tanto al ingreso como a la salida. Simplemente no quisieron escuchar. Luego me mandaron a retirarme de la entrada con un guardia (Sr Guaman). Al cabo de nada, empecé a sufrir una crisis acompañada de taquicardia, llamaron a primeros auxilios, quienes me acompañaron hasta estabilizarme y luego me dejaron entrar. Fue una experiencia terrible. Necesitan más capacitación y empatia, les recuerdo que el mundo está lleno de diversidad cultural, que antes éramos el Tahuantinsuyo y que compartíamos y seguimos compartiendo creencias, tradiciones, lenguas y mucho más!

label: neutro

It's nice and quite crowded with tourists and people trying to sell you things.

text_clean: De reserva solo tiene el nombre y lo que te cobran en la entrada. Apenas hay carteles informativos sobre las especies y su estado de conservación, época de cría o paso por la reserva. Hay pescadores pescando con red, varias personas retirando las algas de la playa ( no sé si es por turismo o pesca) que son ecosistemas super importantes para mucha fauna que vive en ellas. Las carreteras dan lástima, los carros y bugys se meten en la playa y por las dunas ( donde viven y anidan muchas especies) y para colmo el centro de interpretación está cerrado por "mantenimiento" . El baño tampoco está incluido en el precio de la entrada. Una vergüenza de reserva, solo es un saca dinero para los turistas. Los paisajes y las playas son muy bonitas y hay muchas aves en la zona

text_clean: This is a fantastic and easily accessible archaeological site in Lima. You have to access the site via a tour, but the tour is worth the time. I found it very informative and a nice introduction to pre-Inca Peruvian culture. It's amazing that the city has basically been built on top of some of the site, and lucky that preservation started before too much was destroyed forever. 100% worth a visit when in Lima.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo, estupendo, ...pero la unica tienda qué tiene los derechos de venta de replicas de las piezas del señor de Sipan está desasbastecida, con un bajón de calidad. No había cajas para conservar los artículos como hace 5 años que regrese por otra de las caras orejeras de aquel entonces, y que en estos días son más caras...es decir, subieron los precios, y bajaron la calidad de la orfebreria...entre otros.

text_clean: Entrada súper económica, pero luego entras y el espectáculo es pobrisimo, no han invertido ni medio, imágenes y sonido de pésima calidad, todo el parque está tomado por juegos pagos en muy mal estado (comenzando por la limpieza), las luces de los mismos interfieren con el “espectáculo”. Cuando quieres ir a ver las piletas te quieren hacer otro cobro adicional porque ahora lo han puesto “instagrameable” Los antiguos puestos de comida y postres tradicionales han sido reemplazados por locales de algodón radioactivo y pollo frito. No lo recomiendo.

text_clean: It's a full day trip from Lima and the flight is really only 20 minutes of viewing the lines. The bus and the lines are actually not so bad, but what people forget to consider is that the planes are 6 seaters and are really shaky and induce dizziness so the experience itself is an exercise in trying to squint to see the lines while not vommitting. The town itself has a couple worthwhile eateries, but I wouldn't spend the night other than to break up the trip.

However, before to arrive, in Petit Thouars avenue there are people who want to deviate you to park far away from the park (Washington park) and charge you for it. Don't follow them. They wear reflective vests and kinda look official but they aren't. Park your car inside the complex.

text_clean: It was an amazing experience and although it was very busy it wasn't crowded as you can see in the pictures. Fyi, the pictures do not do the site justice. Its fantastic!! You can walk up if you feel you need a challenge or you can go up in a bus from the town of Machu Picchu. The queue looks horrendous but it moves quickly and takes about half an hour or so. Up at the top there is loo and you can store luggage so you dont have to drag it up. The start is pretty strenuous and steep with about 200 hundred steps straight up (which is more difficult if you've not yet fully acclimatised to the altitude). From then on it's all a bit easier and the views leave you breathless (as well as the altitude). I'd say that you need to bring lots of water with you because you dehydrate quickly in the heat and strong sun (husband and I brought two litres which wasn't enough). You also definitely need sunblock unless you don't mind burning to a crisp. We saw no evidence of mosquitos! It is also pretty normal for it to rain and thunder so you'll need to come prepared for that. You can visit without a guide but I think you'll not get to appreciate the site as much. At the end you can also walk back down to the town or again just take the bus. Again the queue looks bad but moves very quick.

You can walk up if you feel you need a challenge or you can go up in a bus from the town of Machu Picchu. The queue looks horrendous but it moves quickly and takes about half an hour or so. Up at the top there is loo and you can store luggage so you dont have to drag it up. The start is pretty strenuous and steep with about 200 hundred steps straight up (which is more difficult if you've not yet fully acclimatised to the altitude). From then on it's all a bit easier and the views leave you breathless (as well as the altitude). I'd say that you need to bring lots of water with you because you dehydrate quickly in the heat and strong sun (husband and I brought two litres which wasn't enough). You also definitely need sunblock unless you don't mind burning to a crisp. We saw no evidence of mosquitos! It is also pretty normal for it to rain and thunder so you'll need to come prepared for that. You can visit without a guide but I think you'll not get to appreciate the site as much. At the end you can also walk back down to the town or again just take the bus. Again the queue looks bad but moves very quick.

text_clean: Esta junto a la playa " El Raspon" y las caracteristicas son similares, esta ubicada en el Area de conservacion protegida de la peninsula de Paracas ( Dpto. De Ica ). La via de acceso tiene pesima señalizacion y es insegura. Es una limpia playa de arena y agua agradable, el nombre de la playa se debe a la existencia del yacimiento de carbon del cual se servian los lugareños en la antiguedad, hay que decender aprox. 35 mts. Por escalinatas de concreto bien construidas para llegar a la playa , se puede observar fauna marina y aves guaneras, existen formaciones geologicas y cavidades producidas por la erosion marina y vientos , en estas se observan tambien restos fosiles de flora y fauna asi como capas de diferentes etapas de formacion geologica. Por el tema del decenso a la playa; no recomiendo estas playas para las personas con discapacidad en las piernas o de edad muy avanzada. Hay estacionamiento y seguridad
